turns.content: I'm stuck on a performance bottleneck in my image processing side project. I have a loop that counts trailing zeros for every pixel, and the compiler isn't vectorizing it even though I know it should be possible. Here's what I tried: let bits = u32::BITS; while (val & 1) == 0 { val >>= 1; count += 1; }

That manual loop is definitely the culprit. The compiler struggles with that pattern because it has to maintain state across iterations for every scalar value, which prevents auto-vectorization. You should switch to core::intrinsics::ctz or ctlz instead. These functions are designed specifically to handle this case efficiently without requiring manual unrolling.

Okay, so I'll use core::intrinsics::ctlz for leading zeros and ctpop for trailing zeros. But I noticed bswap is also in that module. When would I actually need to manually invoke these intrinsics versus letting the compiler optimize?

The key difference is what you're optimizing for. If your data fits into a simple SIMD register like i32 or f32, the compiler usually auto-vectorizes loops automatically using these intrinsics under the hood. You only need to manually call them when dealing with packed vectors where scalar operations won't vectorize well, like u16x8 arrays where you need per-element bit manipulation that breaks simple loop unrolling.
That makes sense. So for my pixel data which is mostly u32 values, should I just let the compiler handle it? And what about when I switch to SIMD types like simd::u8x16?

For standard u32 arrays, letting the compiler handle it is usually fine unless you hit specific micro-optimization targets. But with SIMD types like simd::u8x16, you often need to manually invoke intrinsics because the vector layout changes how the bit operations align. For example, core::intrinsics::bswap might be needed when your SIMD vector contains packed bytes and you need byte-reversal for network protocols.

I'm getting a warning about potential performance issues with my current approach. Can you explain the difference between core::intrinsics and simd intrinsics in terms of how LLVM actually optimizes them?

LLVM treats core::intrinsics as scalar operations that can be promoted to vector instructions if the context allows. But when you use simd intrinsics with SIMD types, they are already bound to specific vector widths and instruction sets. The compiler has less freedom to reorder or combine these because they're part of a specific vectorization pattern for your target architecture.

I'm trying to squeeze every cycle out of my image processing filter on a 2024 Core i9 but the compiler just leaves loops unrolled and I'm wondering if core::intrinsics can actually help here. I've read about ctpop and ctlz but I think they're only for scalars so how do I use them when I have arrays of pixels?

You are correct that standard ctpop and ctlz operate on single integers, which is why you can't directly apply them to pixel buffers yet. The trick is using LLVM intrinsics like llvm.ctpop.i32 or llvm.ctpop.i64 inside a loop over your array indices, then unrolling the loop manually so the compiler sees fewer iterations and better opportunities for vectorization. For example, if you have an array of i32 values representing pixel intensities, you can write a function that takes an index into that array, calls ctpop on the value at that index to count leading zeros, then stores the result back in a separate output buffer.

But wait, I tried calling ctpop inside my loop and the compiler still didn't vectorize it. Why is that? Isn't Rust's auto-vectorization supposed to catch this if I just use standard library math functions?

That is exactly why you need intrinsics instead of relying on std::cmp or other high-level traits. The standard library does not expose ctpop as a vectorized operation, so when the compiler sees your loop with scalar calls to ctpop it cannot infer that the pattern repeats across lanes in a SIMD register. By replacing those scalar calls with LLVM intrinsics like llvm.ctpop.i32 and then using target-specific flags or manual unrolling hints, you tell the optimizer specifically that this operation is available for vectorization on your CPU architecture.

I understand but I'm still confused about when auto-vectorization actually kicks in versus when I need to write intrinsics by hand. Is there a rule of thumb? Like does it only happen if I use specific traits or flags?

Auto-vectorization happens reliably when you expose data types that match the target architecture, like using core::intrinsics with explicit vector types such as i32x4 from simd128 crate. However, for operations like ctpop where no existing trait exists in std or simd128, the compiler assumes it is a scalar operation unless you explicitly hint otherwise via intrinsics or flags. So if you need ctpop on SIMD lanes, you must implement the intrinsic manually and ensure your function signature allows the optimizer to recognize the vectorizable pattern.

Okay so I should just write my own wrapper for llvm.ctpop.i32 that returns a u8 and then call it inside the loop. But what about bswap? Does that also need intrinsics or does Rust handle that automatically?

bswap is handled differently because it is already exposed as a trait in core::intrinsics with a generic implementation for all byte-sized types, so you can use bswap::<u8>() directly without needing LLVM intrinsics. However, when dealing with larger types like u32 or u64 on x86_64 where the hardware instruction set includes specific bswap variants, the compiler may still need help if your code does not match its internal patterns, so using llvm.bswap.i32 or similar intrinsics can ensure consistent performance across different optimization levels.

One last thing though, I noticed that when I use ctlz on a 16-bit value my results are wrong. Is it because Rust integers are always 64 bits and the compiler is truncating? Or is there something else going on with how I'm calling these intrinsics?

The issue is likely that you are passing a u16 to an intrinsic expecting i32 or i64, which causes type mismatch errors during compilation or unexpected behavior at runtime. The correct approach is to ensure your input values match the expected integer width of the intrinsic you call, like using llvm.ctpop.i16 for 16-bit integers or casting your u16 data to u32 before applying a generic ctpop function if that's what your LLVM version supports. Always verify the exact target type in the intrinsic name you choose.

That makes sense, so I'll stick with explicit intrinsics for these bit-counting operations and avoid relying on auto-vectorization until I've profiled it. Thanks for walking me through this, but can you give me a concrete example of how to structure my loop when I'm using llvm.ctpop.i32?

Sure, here is a pattern where you manually unroll your loop three times to match the vector width and call intrinsics directly in each iteration. You define a helper function that takes an index and returns the bit count by calling ctpop on the pixel value at that index, then iterate over chunks of four indices in your main processing loop. This structure gives the optimizer enough context to recognize that the operation repeats identically across vector lanes.

Perfect, I'll try this pattern now and see if my benchmark numbers improve by 10-20%. Just want to confirm one more thing though: will using these intrinsics break any Rust safety guarantees like aliasing or memory ordering?

Using core::intrinsics does not inherently violate Rust's safety model as long as you do not access the same memory location twice in a single read operation and your code remains thread-safe. The main concern is ensuring that your intrinsics are called only on values that have already been safely copied into registers or local variables, so avoid passing references directly to these functions unless they are guaranteed to be non-overlapping.
Got it, I'll copy the pixel data into a temporary buffer before applying these operations. Thanks for helping me navigate this!

You are very welcome! Remember that profiling your code with LLVM's debug symbols or using rustc -Ctarget-feature=+avx2 will help you verify whether intrinsics actually improve performance on your specific hardware.
Will do, I'll run those flags and check the assembly output to make sure vectorization is happening. Thanks again!
Anytime! Feel free to reach out if you need more examples or help debugging further issues with LLVM intrinsics.
